Most games with this kind of DRM (digital rights management) will check the server for any use of the game, even when there are no online or multiplayer features in the game, allowing them to disable access to the game whenever they choose

paragraphs.text: In the realm of digital fairness, video games suffer from a wide array of issues. Advertising, gambling and social media influencers are not all of them. Another big issue involves publishers who specifically require developers to add additional code that can disable games remotely. Rather than list examples of games already disabled in this way, or games likely soon to be, I will instead be giving an example of a game that does things well. Two Worlds (the first one, Epic Edition on Steam), like many games, has serial keys and online activation. However it only checks or requires those servers for multiplayer features. Should the publishers some day cut support for the online lobby servers and related key activation servers, the singleplayer portion of the game will remain fully playable. In addition the server address of the activation server is stored in the windows registry, allowing them to simply release a minimal activation server at end of life that can run locally (on the users pc). The game also natively includes the option to add new lobby servers, although the official servers are still online (most of the time), and they have not yet release the software for them (likely due to security concerns due to the network protocol used) That (Two Worlds) is a game I consider to be developed in a fair manner. showing that parts which need no servers (offline single player story) can continue to work without them, and that should the publishers end support, they have the option of releasing the software such that the community may continue to support it themselves. Most games with this kind of DRM (digital rights management) will check the server for any use of the game, even when there are no online or multiplayer features in the game, allowing them to disable access to the game whenever they choose or when they are no longer able to maintain such servers. Some companies who develop such DRM only intend its use for the duration of sale of a game (as it is meant to protect sales), but few publishers to my knowledge actually remove it when they stop selling or supporting the game, leading the game to become unplayable by all customers.
